message:
perf(indexer): add machine-responsiveness governor and hoist CPU-heavy
prep out of writer transactions via rayon

Two related throughput/responsiveness changes to the indexing pipeline
that together keep big rebuilds fast without making a shared workstation
feel dead while one is running.

1. `src/indexer/responsiveness.rs` (new) — lightweight capacity governor
   that reads `/proc/loadavg` (normalized by logical CPU count) and
   `/proc/pressure/cpu` `some avg10` to publish an integer capacity
   percentage in `[min_capacity_pct, 100]`. Design goals:
   * Conservative by default — never grows past caller-requested fan-out,
     only shrinks when the host is already under pressure, so behaviour
     on an idle box is unchanged.
   * Explainable — thresholds live in named env vars and the current
     decision is queryable via `current_capacity_pct`.
   * Non-oscillating — shrink is immediate so responsiveness recovers
     fast, but growth back to full capacity requires multiple consecutive
     healthy ticks (hysteresis) so flapping load does not flap the worker
     count.
   * Opt-out — `CASS_RESPONSIVENESS_DISABLE=1` pins capacity at 100% for
     before/after comparison runs and sandboxed environments.
   On non-Linux platforms the reader always reports healthy and the
   governor reduces to a no-op.

2. `src/indexer/mod.rs` — wire the governor into
   `lexical_rebuild_page_prep_worker_parallelism()` via
   `effective_worker_count(desired)` so the lexical rebuild page-prep
   pool respects it, and hoist `map_to_internal` out of
   `persist_conversations_batched`'s writer transaction. That transform
   is pure, allocator-heavy (string clones, optional secret redaction,
   message + snippet remap) and does not depend on any writer-assigned
   id, so there is no reason to hold the SQLite writer lock while we
   burn CPU on it. It now runs in parallel via rayon before the writer
   loop starts, shortening the serial writer-hold window and exploiting
   headroom on many-core hosts. Adds a `serial`-gated regression test
   (`persist_conversations_batched_parallel_pre_map_preserves_content_\
   and_order`) that exercises the heavier allocation path with
   `CASS_REDACT_SECRETS=1` across 32 conversations to assert the
   parallel pre-compute does NOT change persisted content, message
   ordering, or redaction behaviour.

3. `src/indexer/semantic.rs` — parallel canonicalize + content-hash
   prep for the semantic indexer. A new `prepare_window` helper runs
   `canonicalize_for_embedding` + `content_hash` across each batch on
   the rayon thread pool (preserving input order via
   `par_iter().filter_map().collect()`), filters out messages whose
   canonical form is empty so the embedder never sees useless inputs,
   and can be forced back to the original serial loop via
   `CASS_SEMANTIC_PREP_PARALLEL=0` for before/after profiling. Default
   embedder batch size factored out to `DEFAULT_SEMANTIC_BATCH_SIZE =
   128` (with `CASS_SEMANTIC_BATCH_SIZE` override), which is a sweet
   spot for ONNX MiniLM models on modern CPUs — big enough to amortize
   dispatch overhead and saturate the tensor kernels, small enough
   that one batch fits in L2 and memory reservation stays bounded for
   large corpora.

Net effect: indexing stays as fast as before on dedicated boxes, finishes
faster on many-core boxes (pre-map + canonicalize now overlap with writer
I/O instead of serializing behind it), and the foreground shell / search
requests stay responsive under heavy rebuild load.
